figures.caption: Figure 2 Overview of the Image Suite. Diverse scenes and styles are covered, each shown in first-person and generated third-person views.
Table 6 Quantitative comparison on the Third-Person scenarios. Best results are in bold and second best are underlined.

Figure 5 Qualitative comparison across three evaluation dimensions. The top row demonstrates successful video generation (✓), while the bottom row illustrates common failures (✗) in Visual Quality, Control Alignment, and World Consistency.

Figure 1 Overview of WorldMark. Current interactive world models each define their own control interfaces, scenes, and evaluation protocols, making cross-model comparison impossible. WorldMark resolves this fragmentation through three pillars: (a) A curated test suite of 500 cases spanning first- and third-person views with both photorealistic and stylized reference scenes, ensuring broad coverage of real-world evaluation conditions. (b) A unified action-mapping layer that translates a shared action vocabulary—WASD for movement and L/R for yaw rotation—into each model’s native control format (caption prompts, pose parameters, gamepad signals, action vectors, etc.), so that all models receive semantically identical instructions. (c) Under these standard conditions, feeding the same reference image and same action sequence to different models reveals striking quality divergence that proprietary benchmarks obscure.
Figure 3 The 15 standardized action sequences, ranging from elementary translations and rotations to combined and cyclic trajectories.
Table 5 Quantitative comparison on the First-Person Stylized scenario. Best results are in bold and second best are underlined.
Figure 4 Context-aware action selection. A VLM analyzes the initial image to identify physical constraints and selects plausible action sequences from the predefined library.
Table 3 Action-mapping adapters translate the shared WASD+L/R vocabulary into each model’s native control format.

Figure 6 Spearman correlation between human preference rankings and automated metric scores across three World Consistency dimensions. Each point represents an evaluated model. The high ρ values demonstrate a strong positive correlation, indicating our automated metrics closely align with human perceptual judgments.
Table 4 Quantitative comparison on the First-Person Real scenario. Best results are in bold and second best are underlined.
